text:
## Keep Food Safe After a Disaster or Emergency


NOV. 24, 2025

metadata.source_title: Keep Food Safe After a Disaster or Emergency
metadata.section_header: Keep Food Safe After a Disaster or Emergency
metadata.distribution_statement: US Government publication - inherently public domain under 17 U.S.C. 105

text:
## WHAT TO KNOW


Foods may be unsafe to eat after a disaster or emergency.

Prepare for emergencies and take steps during and after power outages, floods, and other emergencies to keep food safe.

If you're unsure if a food is safe to eat after an emergency, throw it out.

metadata.source_title: Keep Food Safe After a Disaster or Emergency
metadata.distribution_statement: US Government publication - inherently public domain under 17 U.S.C. 105

text:
## Why it's important


If you are in a disaster or emergency, take steps to prevent illness from unsafe food. Refrigerated or frozen foods may not be safe to eat after a loss of power. Food that may have had contact with floodwater or storm water may also be unsafe to eat.

metadata.source_title: Keep Food Safe After a Disaster or Emergency
metadata.distribution_statement: US Government publication - inherently public domain under 17 U.S.C. 105

text:
## Prepare for emergencies


Keep appliance thermometers in your refrigerator and freezer. The refrigerator should be at 40°F or below. The freezer should be at 0°F or below.

Freeze containers of water and gel packs to help keep food at 40°F or below.

Have a cooler and frozen gel packs ready in case you have to remove food from the refrigerator to keep it cold.

Buy dry ice or block ice to keep food cold in the refrigerator if you think the power could be out for a long time.

metadata.source_title: Keep Food Safe After a Disaster or Emergency
metadata.distribution_statement: US Government publication - inherently public domain under 17 U.S.C. 105

text:
## Steps to take during a power outage


Keep refrigerator and freezer doors closed.

If the doors stay closed, food will stay safe up to: 4 hours in a refrigerator, 48 hours in a full freezer, 24 hours in a half-full freezer.

If the power has been out for 4 hours, and a cooler and ice are available, put refrigerated perishable foods in the cooler. To keep them at 40°F or below, add ice or a cold source like frozen gel packs.

Food will last in a fridge for 4 hours without power. Frozen food will last 48 hours in a full freezer and 24 hours in a half-full freezer.

metadata.source_title: Keep Food Safe After a Disaster or Emergency
metadata.section_header: Steps to take during a power outage
metadata.distribution_statement: US Government publication - inherently public domain under 17 U.S.C. 105

text:
## Steps to take after a power outage


Never taste food to know if it is safe to eat. When in doubt, throw it out.

Throw out perishable food in your refrigerator (meat, fish, cut fruits and vegetables, eggs, milk, and leftovers) after 4 hours without power or without a cold source like ice. Throw out any food with an unusual smell, color, or texture.

If you have an appliance thermometer in your refrigerator, check to see if it is still at 40 °F or below.

Check temperatures of food kept in coolers or your refrigerator with an added cold source. Throw out food that has thawed or is above 40°.

You can safely refreeze or cook thawed frozen food that still contains ice crystals or is at 40 °F or below.

metadata.source_title: Keep Food Safe After a Disaster or Emergency
metadata.section_header: Steps to take after a power outage
metadata.distribution_statement: US Government publication - inherently public domain under 17 U.S.C. 105
metadata.warning_text: Never taste food to know if it is safe to eat. When in doubt, throw it out.

text:
## Resource


Check this FoodSafety.gov chart for a list of what foods you should throw out and foods you can refreeze.

metadata.source_title: Keep Food Safe After a Disaster or Emergency
metadata.distribution_statement: US Government publication - inherently public domain under 17 U.S.C. 105

text:
## Throw away these foods


Perishable food that has not been refrigerated or frozen properly due to power outages

Food that may have come in contact with floodwater or stormwater

Food with an unusual smell, color, or texture

Food in packages that are not waterproof

Food in cardboard containers, including juice/milk/baby formula boxes

Food containers with screw caps, snap lids, crimped caps, twist caps, flip tops, and snap tops

Home-canned foods and canned foods or food containers that are bulging, open, or damaged

Throw out cans or food containers that spurt liquid or foam when you open them or contain food that is discolored, moldy, or smells bad

metadata.source_title: Keep Food Safe After a Disaster or Emergency
metadata.distribution_statement: US Government publication - inherently public domain under 17 U.S.C. 105

text:
## When in doubt, throw it out!


Unsafe food can make you sick even if it looks and smells normal.

metadata.source_title: Keep Food Safe After a Disaster or Emergency
metadata.section_header: When in doubt, throw it out!
metadata.distribution_statement: US Government publication - inherently public domain under 17 U.S.C. 105
metadata.warning_text: Unsafe food can make you sick even if it looks and smells normal.

text:
## Clean and sanitize food-contact surfaces that have been flooded


Throw out wooden cutting boards, baby bottle nipples, and pacifiers if they have come into contact with floodwaters. Sanitizing methods are not effective for removing contaminates in floodwater from these items.

Clean and sanitize dishes, utensils, and other surfaces that touch food (like refrigerator drawers or kitchen countertops) in a four-step process:

metadata.source_title: Keep Food Safe After a Disaster or Emergency
metadata.section_header: Clean and sanitize food-contact surfaces that have been flooded
metadata.distribution_statement: US Government publication - inherently public domain under 17 U.S.C. 105
metadata.warning_text: Sanitizing methods are not effective for removing contaminates in floodwater from these items.

text:
## Steps to take


1 Wash

Wash with hot, soapy water.

2 Rinse

Rinse with clean, safe water.

3 Sanitize

Make a solution of 1 cup (8 oz/240 mL) of unscented household chlorine bleach in 5 gallons of clean water. Soak items for 1 minute in the beach solution (solution should completely cover the items). For items that you can't put in the solution (like countertops), apply solution with a cloth.

4 Dry Allow to air dry.

How to save commercially prepared food

This includes food in cans, plastic, or metal pouches (like flexible, shelf stable juice packages).

metadata.source_title: Keep Food Safe After a Disaster or Emergency
metadata.distribution_statement: US Government publication - inherently public domain under 17 U.S.C. 105

text:
## Steps to take


1 Remove labels

Remove labels if possible. Note the expiration date.


Wipe away dirt

Brush or wipe away dirt or silt.

3 Wash

Wash cans and pouches with hot, soapy water.

4 Rinse

Rinse cans and pouches with clean, safe water.

5 Sanitize

Sanitize cans and pouches in one of two ways: Place them in a solution of 1 cup (8 oz/240 mL) of unscented household bleach in 5 gallons of water for 15 minutes, OR put them in a pot of water, bring to a boil, and continue boiling for 2 minutes.

6 Re-label

Re-label cans or pouches with a marker. Include the expiration date.

7 Use

Use food in cans or pouches as soon as possible.
